items.correct: No - affirmative 'creo que' takes the indicative; the subjunctive appears when the opinion is negated, as in 'no creo que sea'.
items.misconception: All opinion phrases automatically trigger the subjunctive.
items.random_wrong: The subjunctive is only used in written Spanish, never in speech.
items.why: The subjunctive marks doubt or denial: negating the belief introduces uncertainty, while affirming it does not, so the two structures take different moods.

items.q: Do long memorised monologues impress the examiner most?
items.correct: No - the unit rewards spontaneous interaction: developing ideas, justifying opinions and responding naturally to follow-up questions.
items.misconception: Reciting prepared paragraphs perfectly is the key to top marks.
items.random_wrong: Marks are awarded for speaking as fast as possible.
items.why: Speaking assessments test communication, and examiners ask unpredictable questions precisely to move candidates beyond rehearsed material.

items.correct: The subjunctive: 'Es importante que los jóvenes voten.'
items.misconception: It takes the indicative because it states a fact: 'es importante que votan'.
items.random_wrong: It must be followed by an infinitive with no subject.
items.why: Impersonal value judgements (es importante/necesario/mejor que) trigger the subjunctive in the subordinate clause, a structure that lifts speaking marks when used accurately.

items.q: Which verb form correctly completes 'No creo que ___ justo'?
items.why: It's sea. “es” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: However. “Without luggage” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If you forget a Spanish word during the speaking exam, the best strategy is to do what?
items.correct: Paraphrase the idea using Spanish you do know
items.random_wrong: Stay silent until the examiner moves on
items.why: Correct answer: Paraphrase the idea using Spanish you do know. “Say the word in English” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: 'Por un lado... por otro lado' is used to do what?
items.correct: Contrast two sides of an argument
items.why: Correct answer: Contrast two sides of an argument. “Give directions in the street” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
